user_request: Draft a commercial suit seeking damages for illegal termination of dealership agreement. Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed territory market. Termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. Claim compensation for loss of profit, goodwill and unsold stock. Draft with proper breach of contract pleading.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks damages for illegal termination of a commercial dealership agreement. The plaintiff invested capital and developed the territory, but claims the termination was arbitrary and breached contract terms. Compensation is sought for loss of profit, goodwill, and unsold stock.

130. Under s 8 of the Suits Valuation Act, 1887, the value of the subject matter of the suit is Rs 130. The parties 
arrive at a compromise whereby the first defendant is to pay Rs 6,000 and the second defendant Rs 5,000 to 
the plaintiff. The court may pass a decree in terms of the compromise, although it has jurisdiction limited to suits 
of which the value of the subject matter does not exceed Rs 5,000.14

legal_research.summary:
[Statute of limitations in contract claims : an overview - iPleaders] Under normal circumstances, the Indian Limitation Act of 1963, under Section 14 of the Limitation Act, provides that a claim for breach of contract must be made within three years from the date of such breach .
[Applicabilty of Limitation Act to Indian Contract Act] In other words, if there is no specific provision in the Limitation Act or any other law that prescribes a limitation period for a particular type of suit, then the plaintiff can file such a suit within three years from the date when the right ...
[The Period of Limitation For A Suit For Compensation For The Breach of Any Contract | PDF | Breach Of Contract | Damages] It is three years from the date the contract was broken, or from the date of the most recent breach if there were successive breaches, or from the date the continuing breach ceased .
[1 Limitation Periods: Overview (India)] Limitation Act prescribes separate periods for the preference of suit in tort, and for breach of · obligations under contract.
[Dhapai vs Dalla And Ors. on 2 April, 1969] Three years When the contract is broken, or (where there are successive breaches) when the breach in respect of which the suit is instituted occurs or (where the breach is continuing) when it ceases . 120. Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this Schedule.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] Order IV, Rule 1 of the CPC mandates that “ Every suit shall be instituted by presenting a plaint in duplicate to the Court or such officer as it appoints in this behalf ” and that “Every plaint shall comply with the rules contained in Orders ...
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] During the tenure of his license, Dogra alleged breaches of contractual stipulations by the Excise Authorities, leading him to serve a notice under Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) and subsequently file a suit claiming damages.
[Revisiting The Principles Of Damages Under The Contract Regime In India - Contracts and Commercial Law - India] The Supreme Court in M.N. Gangappa v. Atmakur Nagabhushanam Shetty & Co. and Anr., (AIR 1972 SC 696) has observed that in absence of a strict procedure for calculation of damages arising out of breach of contract, damages would be calculated based on the facts and circumstances of each case.
[Documents Filed with Plaint: Comprehensive Legal Framework and Judicial Interpretation - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The judgment highlighted that allowing parties to introduce documents at later stages without proper justification would undermine the procedural integrity of civil suits and potentially prejudice the opposing party’s ability to prepare an adequate defense. The decision also clarified that the requirement to file documents with the plaint is not merely directory but mandatory, meaning that non-compliance carries substantive consequences.

[The basic court procedure for filing a civil suit in India | SoOLEGAL] File requisite amount of procedure - fee in the court. File 2 copies of plaint for each defendant in the court . Of, the 2 copies for each defendant, one shall be sent by Register/post/courier, and one by Ordinary post.
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] The Supreme Court examined whether Amar Nath Dogra’s suit against the Union of India complied with the procedural requirements of Section 80 CPC , which mandates that a notice must be served before initiating a lawsuit against the government.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The plaint thus functions as both a procedural requirement and a substantive pleading that shapes the contours of litigation from inception to resolution. Section 26 of the CPC establishes the fundamental principle governing the institution of civil suits, providing that “Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be prescribed.” This provision underscores the mandatory nature of the plaint in civil litigation, making it an indispensable prerequisite for the valid institution of any civil suit in Indian courts.

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_TYPE}} AT {{PLACE}}

O.S. No. __________ / 20____ — DAMAGES PLAINT FOR ILLEGAL TERMINATION OF DEALERSHIP AGREEMENT

PLAINTIFF: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.                                                        ... DEFENDANT


JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is identifiable as {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} and is capable of being sued.\n2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try the present suit as the Defendant carries on dealership activities within the local limits and the cause of action arose within such limits. This Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to entertain the suit as the claim for damages falls within the pecuniary limits prescribed. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits of this nature.\n3. The Court has jurisdiction to entertain the present suit under the applicable provisions and within the territorial and pecuniary limits related to the cause of action.


FACTS OF THE CASE

2. The Plaintiff and Defendant entered into a dealership agreement on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (a true copy annexed as Annexure A).
3. The Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed the Territory in reliance on the said dealership agreement.
4. The Defendant terminated the dealership on {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, which termination was arbitrary and contrary to the express terms of the dealership agreement and the Plaintiff's performance thereunder.
5. The Plaintiff served a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} through registered post A.D., demanding {{NOTICE_DEMAND}} within 15 days, a true copy of which is annexed as Annexure C.
6. Despite receipt of the said legal notice, the Defendant has failed and neglected to rectify the breach or reinstate the dealership, thereby necessitating the filing of the present suit.
7. The Plaintiff seeks damages for loss of profits, diminution of goodwill, and for unsold stock resulting from the unlawful termination.
8. The Plaintiff has complied with mandatory pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015; a Mediation Certificate under Section 12A is annexed as Annexure D.


LEGAL BASIS

9. The suit is maintainable under Section 26 CPC inasmuch as the Plaintiff seeks relief for breach of contract and damages arising therefrom.\n12. The mandatory pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 has been complied with; a Mediation CertificateUnder Section 12A is annexed as Annexure D.\n13. The limitation for compensation for breach of contract is governed by Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, prescribing a period of three years; the cause of action accrued on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} and the suit is within such period.


CAUSE OF ACTION

10. The cause of action first arose on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} upon termination of the dealership agreement and is a continuing cause of action which subsists and continues till realization of the suit amount.\n15. The breach of the dealership agreement by the Defendant entitled the Plaintiff to damages for loss of profits, goodwill, and unsold stock, as claimed in the prayers.


LIMITATION

11. Limitation: Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. Period: Three years. Reason: For compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied, not herein specially provided for; accrual from the date of breach ({{TERMINATION_DATE}}).


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

12. The suit is valued at Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{DAMAGES_WORDS}} Only) for purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint, payable as per COURT FEE CONTEXT.


INTEREST

13. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:\n   (a) Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the filing of this suit;\n   (b) Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum during the pendency of this suit;\n   (c) Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till actual realization.


PRAYER

14. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:\n   (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for the sum of Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{DAMAGES_WORDS}} Only) together with interest as claimed hereinabove;\n   (b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing;\n   (c) Award pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable;\n   (d) Award future interest from the date of decree till the date of actual realization;\n   (e) Award costs of this suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for issuing legal notice;\n   (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Sr. No. 1. Dealership Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} — Annexure A\nSr. No. 2. Proof of investment and development of Territory — Annexure B\nSr. No. 3. Legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} with postal receipt and A.D. — Annexure C\nSr. No. 4. Mediation Certificate under Section 12A, Commercial Courts Act, 2015 — Annexure D


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 21 of this plaint are true to my personal knowledge and belief, that no part of the cause of action has arisen in any other court, and that I have not concealed any material facts. Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.\n\n                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}\n                                                        PLAINTIFF\n\nThrough:\n{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}\nAdvocate\nEnrollment No. {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}\n{{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
